# Scripts That Fail Loudly

Everything so far has been you at the prompt: you type a
command, read what it prints, and decide what to do next. A
pipeline you mean to run again, on a server, next year, or by a
collaborator, cannot lean on you being there to read the
output. It has to be a *script*, a file of commands that runs
unattended, and a script that runs unattended carries two
obligations the interactive prompt let you skip. It must *fail
loudly*: stop the instant a step goes wrong instead of marching
on and writing a plausible-looking wrong answer. And it must
*reproduce identically*: give the same result on your laptop, a
Linux server, and a GPU pod, instead of quietly depending on
which machine ran it. This chapter is those two habits, and
they are close cousins, because both come down to refusing to
let the shell paper over a problem.

This is where several promises from earlier chapters come due.
Chapter 7 showed `pipefail` and promised that the book's scripts
would open with `set -euo pipefail`; here they do. Chapter 2
introduced the exit code and `$?` and deferred `set -e` to here;
this chapter makes them operational. Chapter 3 took a firm
position, write scripts for bash with portable habits, and
flagged the macOS bash-3.2 floor; here that becomes the rule a
script is written to. Chapter 8 pinned `LC_ALL` for a
reproducible `sort` and left the standard script header to this
chapter. And the loud, reproducible script you build here is the
thin wrapper the Make graph of Chapter 12 will invoke: Make owns
the dependency graph, and a script is the small, honest step at
each node.

A note on where the output comes from. The shebang, `set -euo
pipefail`, exit codes, the `trap`, the `set -e` footguns,
`bash -x`, `tee`, `/usr/bin/time -v`, the `TZ` and `LC_ALL`
pinning, and the `verify.sh` anchor are executed in this book's
Linux sandbox (Ubuntu 22.04.5, GNU bash 5.1.16, GNU coreutils
8.32, util-linux 2.37.2; the `ch10-*` transcripts), in throwaway
`/tmp` scratch directories. The `if`/`[[ ]]`/`for`/`while` primer
and `{ ...; }` grouping are captured the same way in
`ch10-primer.txt`; that capture used a newer sandbox image
(bash 5.2.21). Its shown output carries no version-dependent
values (no version strings, no locale- or build-sensitive
formatting), so re-capturing on the 22.04/bash 5.1.16 image of
the other `ch10-*` files is expected to reproduce it, though
that was not done. Four things run on the author's Mac
(`transcripts/capture-ch10-mac.sh`) because the sandbox cannot
produce them: ShellCheck and shfmt (blocked release binaries,
like DuckDB in Chapter 8 and `fd` in Chapter 9), the macOS
bash-3.2 failures, and BSD `/usr/bin/time` and `date`. Those
Mac blocks are marked where they appear. No real credential or
account name appears anywhere; scratch paths are relative.

## The shebang and the bash target {.tier-beginner}

A script is just a text file of commands, but the kernel needs
to be told which interpreter should read it. That is the job of
the *shebang*, the first line, which begins with `#!` followed
by the interpreter to run. Write it as `#!/usr/bin/env bash`:

```bash
$ cat pipeline.sh
#!/usr/bin/env bash
echo "running under bash $BASH_VERSION"
$ chmod +x pipeline.sh
$ ./pipeline.sh
running under bash 5.1.16(1)-release
```

Three things make that work. The `#!/usr/bin/env bash` line
tells the kernel to find `bash` on your `PATH` and feed it the
file, so the script runs under bash wherever bash happens to be
installed. The `chmod +x` (Chapter 10) marks the file executable
so you can run it as `./pipeline.sh`. And the script reports the
bash that actually ran it, `5.1.16` in the sandbox. Why
`/usr/bin/env bash` rather than a hardcoded `#!/bin/bash`? A
Homebrew or Linuxbrew bash lives at `/opt/homebrew/bin/bash` or
`/home/linuxbrew/.../bash`, not `/bin/bash`; `env` searches
your `PATH` and finds whichever one you installed, so the same
shebang works on a Mac and a server.

The one shebang to avoid for a research script is
`#!/bin/sh`. On Debian and Ubuntu `/bin/sh` is `dash`, a lean
POSIX shell that does not have the bash features this chapter
relies on for safety, `set -o pipefail`, `[[ ]]`, and arrays
among them. Pointing a script at `/bin/sh` and then writing
bash inside it is how a script works on your Mac (where
`/bin/sh` quietly runs in a bash-compatible mode) and breaks on
a Linux server. Pick bash, say so in the shebang, and write
portable habits inside; do not write zsh in a script either,
which is fine at your interactive prompt but is not the target
you automate against.

::: {.callout-note .divergence title="DIVERGENCE"}
"Write for bash" hides a version trap that Chapter 3 flagged and
this chapter must make operational. macOS ships a *system* bash
frozen at 3.2.57 (2007), while Linux and WSL2 run bash 5.x (the
sandbox's 5.1.16 above). A script that uses a bash-4-or-later
feature runs on the server and fails on a Mac's `/bin/bash`.
The usual casualties are associative arrays (`declare -A`),
uppercase/lowercase expansion (`${var^^}`), and `mapfile` /
`readarray`. Asking the system bash for an associative array
shows the floor (captured on the author's Mac,
`transcripts/ch10-bash32-mac.txt`):

```bash
$ /bin/bash --version
GNU bash, version 3.2.57(1)-release (arm64-apple-darwin25)
$ /bin/bash -c 'declare -A m; m[x]=1; echo ${m[x]}'
/bin/bash: line 0: declare: -A: invalid option
declare: usage: declare [-afFirtx] [-p] [name[=value] ...]
1
```

The version line confirms the 3.2.57 floor, and `declare -A`
is rejected as an invalid option. Worse than the plain error,
bash 3.2 then reads `m[x]=1` as an ordinary *indexed*
assignment, so the last `echo` prints `1`: a script that asked
for an associative array does not get one and marches on with a
different data structure than you wrote. The fix is not to write
to the 3.2 floor forever; it is to
`brew install bash` (Chapter 3) so a modern bash sits on your
`PATH`, and let `#!/usr/bin/env bash` find it. The *system*
`/bin/bash` stays at 3.2 no matter what you install, which is
exactly why the shebang points at `env bash` and not `/bin/bash`.
When a script must also run under the bare system bash, avoid
the bash-4 features above; when it need not, install a current
bash and target it.
:::

## Fail loudly: `set -euo pipefail` {.tier-beginner}

By default a shell script keeps going after a command fails. It
runs the next line, and the next, whatever happened before, so a
failure in step one becomes a wrong answer in step five with no
sign anything went wrong. Three shell options turn that off, and
together they are the first line of nearly every serious script.
Put them at the top:

```bash
$ cat step.sh
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail
echo "step 1: clean"
cp missing.csv clean.csv    # fails: file not found
echo "step 2: never runs"
$ ./step.sh
step 1: clean
cp: cannot stat 'missing.csv': No such file or directory
$ echo $?
1
```

With `set -e` in force, the failing `cp` stops the script then
and there: "step 2" never prints, and the script's exit status
is the failing command's `1`, so anything that runs it, a
Makefile, a scheduler, another script, sees the failure. That is
the `-e`: *exit on error*. The `-o pipefail` is the option
Chapter 7 introduced, which makes a pipeline fail when any stage
fails, not only its last; you carry it here as part of the
header rather than re-deriving it. The `-u` is *treat an unset
variable as an error*, which catches the most common typo in a
script, a misspelled or never-set variable name:

```bash
$ cat need.sh
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail
echo "output dir is [${OUTDIR}]"
$ ./need.sh
./need.sh: line 3: OUTDIR: unbound variable
```

Without `-u` that line would print `output dir is []` and sail
on, and a later `rm -rf "$OUTDIR"/*` (Chapter 7) would expand to
`rm -rf /*`. With `-u` the script stops at the unset variable
and names the line. Read `set -euo pipefail` as one habit: stop
on the first failed command, stop on an unset variable, and stop
on a failure anywhere in a pipe. Type it at the top of every
script before you write anything else.

::: {.callout-caution .danger title="DANGER"}
A script *without* `set -euo pipefail` does not announce its
failures; it commits them to disk. Watch a step whose input is
missing sail straight past the error and write a confident,
empty result:

```bash
$ cat summarize.sh
#!/usr/bin/env bash
sort data/clean.csv > sorted.csv
wc -l < sorted.csv > count.txt
echo "summary written"
$ ./summarize.sh          # data/clean.csv does not exist
sort: cannot read: data/clean.csv: No such file or directory
summary written
$ echo $?
0
$ cat count.txt
0
```

`sort` failed, but the `>` had already truncated `sorted.csv` to
an empty file (Chapter 7), `wc` happily counted its zero lines,
and the script printed "summary written" and *exited 0*. Nothing
looks wrong: there is an output file, there is a success status,
and a downstream step or a collaborator has no reason to
suspect the count is a lie. This is the scripted sibling of
Chapter 7's `pipefail` trap and Chapter 6's raw-in/generated-out
hygiene: a silent failure is worse than a loud one, because you
act on it. Adding `set -euo pipefail` to the top of this script
turns the missing input into an immediate nonzero exit, which is
the whole point of the previous block.
:::

## Reading a script: tests, branches, and loops {.tier-beginner}

The scripts in this chapter, and the Make pipeline of Chapter
12, lean on a small grammar that reads an exit code, branches
on it, and repeats a step over a list. Chapter 4 showed how to
read a single command; this section reads the constructs that
turn a list of commands into a script, and every one of them
rests on the exit code from Chapter 2.

Start with that exit code, because it is the value all the
branching acts on. Every command returns a code, zero for
success and nonzero for failure, and `$?` holds the most recent
one. In a script the code is not just a report you read
afterward; it is the condition the shell tests. `grep -q` shows
it cleanly, printing nothing and only setting the code, zero
when the pattern is found and nonzero when it is not:

```bash
$ grep -q F0000 firms.txt; echo $?
0
$ grep -q F9999 firms.txt; echo $?
1
```

`F0000` is in the file so the first `grep` exits 0; the absent
`F9999` exits 1. That 0-or-1 is what the next constructs branch
on.

An `if` runs a command and takes a branch on its exit code. The
shape is `if COMMAND; then ...; else ...; fi`, and `COMMAND` is
really run: exit 0 takes the `then` branch, anything else takes
the `else`. There is no separate boolean type; the exit code is
the condition:

```bash
$ cat check.sh
#!/usr/bin/env bash
if grep -q F0000 firms.txt; then
  echo "found F0000"
else
  echo "no F0000"
fi
$ ./check.sh
found F0000
```

`grep -q` succeeded, so the `then` branch ran. `elif` adds
another condition, `else` is the fallthrough, and `fi` (`if`
spelled backward) closes the block. The one token you cannot
drop is the `;` or newline before `then`.

Most conditions are not a command like `grep` but a test: does
this file exist, is this string empty, is this number bigger
than that one. bash's test keyword is `[[ ... ]]`, and like any
command it sets an exit code you can read on its own:

```bash
$ [[ -f factors.csv ]]; echo $?
0
$ [[ -f missing.csv ]]; echo $?
1
$ [[ 180 -gt 100 ]]; echo $?
0
$ [[ F0000 == F* ]]; echo $?
0
```

`-f` asks whether a regular file exists (0 yes, 1 no); `-gt`
compares two integers, with `-lt`, `-le`, `-ge`, `-eq`, and
`-ne` for the other five; `==` matches a string against a glob
pattern, so `F0000` matches `F*`. The tests you reach for most
are `-f` (file), `-d` (directory), `-z` (empty string), and
`-n` (non-empty), and a leading `!` negates any of them, so
`[[ ! -f x ]]` is true whenever `x` is not a regular file,
whether it is missing or exists as something else (a directory,
say). Dropped into an `if`, that is the guard the next section
builds: `if [[ ! -f data/clean.csv ]]; then ...`.

You will also meet the older single-bracket test `[ -f file ]`
in scripts, and its spaces are load-bearing. The `[` is not
punctuation; it is an actual command, a synonym for `test`, and
the `]` is a required final argument. Because `[` is a command,
it needs a space after it and before the `]`, or the shell
never finds the word boundaries:

```bash
$ [ -f factors.csv ]; echo $?
0
$ [ -f factors.csv]; echo $?
bash: [: missing `]'
2
```

The spaced form works; remove the space before `]` and `[`
never sees its closing argument. This book writes `[[ ]]`
instead: it is a shell keyword rather than a command, so it
does not word-split its contents (an unquoted empty variable
can turn `[ $x = y ]` into a syntax error, while `[[ ]]`
tolerates it), it accepts `==` patterns and `&&`/`||`, and it
behaves the same in bash and zsh. Write `[[ ]]`; recognize
`[ ]` when you read it, and mind its spaces.

To repeat a step over a list, `for NAME in LIST; do ...; done`
runs its body once per item with `NAME` bound to each. With
semicolons between the parts it fits on one line:

```bash
$ for id in F0000 F0001 F0002; do echo "row for $id"; done
row for F0000
row for F0001
row for F0002
```

The `;` before `do` and before `done` are what let the loop sit
on one line; you can also type it across several lines, pressing
Enter after `do`. The list is usually a glob rather than typed
ids, so `for f in *.csv; do ...; done` walks the matching files,
and inside the body you quote the variable as `"$f"` for the
reason Chapter 7 makes central: an unquoted `$f` word-splits on
spaces.

Where `for` walks a known list, `while TEST; do ...; done`
repeats as long as its test keeps succeeding. A counter is the
plain shape:

```bash
$ n=1
$ while [[ $n -le 3 ]]; do echo "attempt $n"; n=$((n+1)); done
attempt 1
attempt 2
attempt 3
```

`$(( ))` does the integer arithmetic (Chapter 4). The form you
reach for most on data is `while read`, which streams a file
one line at a time until it runs out. The command below is
wrapped across lines with a trailing `\`, the continuation the
book uses for any command too wide for the page:

```bash
$ while IFS= read -r id; do \
      echo "processing $id"; \
  done < firms.txt
processing F0000
processing F0001
processing F0002
```

`read` pulls one line per pass and stops at end of file; the
`IFS=` prefix and `-r` flag keep leading spaces and backslashes
intact, the safe defaults for reading real data.

One last construct ties the loops back to redirection. Braces
run several commands as a unit so a single redirection covers
all of them. Chapter 4's bracket table introduced `{ ...; }` as
command grouping; in a script its everyday job is to send a
whole block's output to one place:

```bash
$ { echo "line 1"; echo "line 2"; } > grouped.log
$ cat grouped.log
line 1
line 2
```

Both lines land in `grouped.log` because the `>` applies to the
group, not just the last `echo`. Two bits of punctuation are
load-bearing: `{` must be followed by a space, and a `;` or a
newline (not a space) must come before the closing `}`.
Chapter 7 used this exact form to capture a pipeline's output
and errors in one log; now the braces are not a mystery.

Those are the moving parts the rest of this chapter and the
Make pipeline are built from: a test sets an exit code, `if`
branches on it, `for` and `while` repeat, and `{ ...; }`
groups. The next section puts the first two to work in a guard
that stops a script before it touches missing data.

## Exit codes you can act on: `exit`, `$?`, and `trap` {.tier-beginner}

The primer read an exit code with `$?` and branched on it with
`if`; here the move is from *reading* a code to *setting* it.
When your script detects a problem it should
stop with a nonzero `exit` and a message on stderr, so its
caller learns something went wrong and, if you choose your
numbers, what:

```bash
$ cat guard.sh
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail
if [[ ! -f data/clean.csv ]]; then
  echo "error: data/clean.csv is missing" >&2
  exit 3
fi
echo "found input"
$ ./guard.sh
error: data/clean.csv is missing
$ echo $?
3
```

The guard checks its precondition, writes the complaint to
stderr with `>&2` (Chapter 7) so it does not pollute the data on
stdout, and `exit 3` ends the script with a status you chose. A
plain `exit` (or the end of the script) returns the last
command's status; `exit 0` forces success; `exit N` reports a
specific failure. For the unexpected failures you did not write
a guard for, a `trap` on the `ERR` condition runs a handler the
moment any command fails, which is where you put a diagnostic or
a cleanup:

```bash
$ cat traps.sh
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail
trap 'echo "failed at line $LINENO (exit $?)" >&2' ERR
echo "checking inputs"
wc -l data/clean.csv
echo "done"
$ ./traps.sh
checking inputs
wc: data/clean.csv: No such file or directory
failed at line 5 (exit 1)
```

The `wc` on a missing file failed, `set -e` began the exit, and
on the way out the `ERR` trap fired and reported the line and
the status; "done" never ran. A trap on `EXIT` (rather than
`ERR`) runs on *every* exit and is the place to delete a scratch
file or a lock whether the script succeeds or fails, the
scripted form of the cleanup Chapter 10's `SIGTERM` handler did
for a signal. Keep the handler short; its job is to leave the
workspace clean and the failure visible, not to soldier on.

::: {.callout-warning .pitfall title="PITFALL"}
`set -e` has famous blind spots, and a script that trusts it too
much can still march past a failure. It does *not* fire when the
failing command's status is consumed by something else. The
sharpest case is a failure hidden inside a `local` assignment,
because `local` is itself a command that succeeds:

```bash
$ cat footgun.sh
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -e
count_rows() {
  local n=$(wc -l < missing.csv)   # command fails...
  echo "count_rows kept going, n=[$n]"
}
count_rows
echo "main continued past set -e"
$ ./footgun.sh
./footgun.sh: line 4: missing.csv: No such file or directory
count_rows kept going, n=[]
main continued past set -e
```

The `wc` failed, but `local` reported its own success, so `set
-e` saw nothing to act on: the function set `n` to empty and the
script continued. Two more contexts disable `set -e` the same
way: a function called as an `if` condition (or on either side
of `&&`/`||`) runs to the end with `set -e` suppressed
throughout, and the left side of `&&` is allowed to fail:

```bash
$ cat footgun2.sh
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -e
f() { false; echo "  f kept going"; }
if f; then echo "  if-branch"; fi
false && echo "unreached"
echo "main continued past set -e"
$ ./footgun2.sh
  f kept going
  if-branch
main continued past set -e
```

The habit that survives these: split the assignment from the
command (`local n; n=$(wc -l < f)`), check the status you care
about explicitly rather than assuming `set -e` caught it, and
lean on the `ERR` trap and ShellCheck (next section) to flag
what the eye misses. `set -euo pipefail` is a floor, not a
force field.
:::

::: {.callout-tip .recovery title="RECOVERY"}
When a script fails and the message alone does not tell you
where, rerun it under `bash -x`, which prints each command,
expanded, as it executes, so you can watch the values and see
exactly which line broke:

```bash
$ cat pipeline_step.sh
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail
infile=data/clean.csv
n=$(wc -l < "$infile")
echo "rows: $n"
$ bash -x pipeline_step.sh
+ set -euo pipefail
+ infile=data/clean.csv
++ wc -l
pipeline_step.sh: line 4: data/clean.csv: No such file or directory
+ n=
```

The `+` lines are the trace: you can see `infile` was set, then
line 4's `wc` tried to read a file that is not there, so `n`
came out empty and the script stopped. `bash -x script.sh` runs
a copy under the trace without editing the file; `set -x` inside
the script turns it on for a region and `set +x` off again. Read
the last `+` line before the error and you have the failing
command with its real arguments.
:::

## Lint before you run: ShellCheck and shfmt {.tier-beginner}

A script that fails loudly at runtime is good; a script whose
bugs are caught *before* it runs is better. **ShellCheck** is a
static analyzer for shell scripts: it reads the file and flags
the mistakes that bite, most of all the unquoted expansion
Chapter 7 warned about. Run it on a script with a bare `$infile`
(captured on the author's Mac, `transcripts/ch10-lint-mac.txt`):

```bash
$ cat unsafe.sh
#!/usr/bin/env bash
infile=$1
wc -l $infile
$ shellcheck unsafe.sh

In unsafe.sh line 3:
wc -l $infile
      ^-----^ SC2086 (info): Double quote to prevent globbing and word splitting.

Did you mean:
wc -l "$infile"

For more information:
  https://www.shellcheck.net/wiki/SC2086 -- Double quote to prevent globbing ...
```

`SC2086` is the code for the unquoted `$infile`, the exact bug
that turns a filename with a space into two arguments; ShellCheck
points at it, explains it, and suggests the fix. You do not need
the whole rule catalogue: the handful a researcher hits
repeatedly are `SC2086` (quote your expansions), the `[ ]`
versus `[[ ]]` test warnings, and the ones about a masked exit
status like the `local` footgun above. Run `shellcheck
yourscript.sh` before you trust a script, and wire it into the
Makefile if you want it enforced.

**shfmt** is the companion formatter: it rewrites a script into
a consistent style so diffs stay small and structure is obvious.
`shfmt -d` shows what it would change without touching the file:

```bash
$ cat messy.sh
#!/usr/bin/env bash
if [ -f data.csv ];then
echo "found"
fi
$ shfmt -d messy.sh
diff messy.sh.orig messy.sh
--- messy.sh.orig
+++ messy.sh
@@ -1,4 +1,4 @@
 #!/usr/bin/env bash
-if [ -f data.csv ];then
-echo "found"
+if [ -f data.csv ]; then
+	echo "found"
 fi
```

It spaces `;then` into `; then` and indents the body with a tab.
`shfmt -w script.sh` writes the changes in place. The two tools
divide the work cleanly: ShellCheck flags the *likely bugs* (it
does not prove a script correct), shfmt makes the layout
*consistent*, and neither runs your code, so both are safe to
run on a script you are still unsure of. Both are third-party installs (`brew install
shellcheck shfmt`; ShellCheck 0.11.0 and shfmt 3.13.1 here,
current as of 2026-07-03), not in every environment, so treat
them as a local pre-flight rather than something the server is
guaranteed to have.

## Leave a run log you can trust {.tier-advanced}

A long job that printed its progress and then scrolled away
leaves you nothing to point to when you ask, later, what it
actually did. The lightweight fix is to keep a log. `tee` reads
a stream and writes it to a file *and* to the screen at once,
delivering the promise Chapter 7 made when it split output with
`>` alone:

```bash
$ ./report_step.sh | tee run.log
loading factors
forming portfolio
done
$ cat run.log
loading factors
forming portfolio
done
```

You watched the run live and it was saved. In practice you give
the log a dated name, `... | tee "logs/run-$(date -u
+%Y%m%dT%H%M%SZ).log"`, so successive runs do not overwrite each
other and the filename records when the job ran. To capture
errors as well as output, merge the streams first: `./job.sh
2>&1 | tee run.log` (Chapter 7). For a heavier record, `script`
captures an entire terminal session, every command and its
output and the timing, into a `typescript` file: `script
session.log` starts recording and `exit` stops it. `script` logs
the whole session where `tee` logs one command's stream, and it
depends on a real terminal, so `tee` is the everyday choice and
`script` is for when you want the complete session on the record.

To learn how long a step took and how much memory it used, put
`/usr/bin/time -v` in front of it (the full path, because bare
`time` is a shell keyword with a terser report):

```bash
$ /usr/bin/time -v sleep 0.2
	Command being timed: "sleep 0.2"
	User time (seconds): 0.00
	System time (seconds): 0.00
	Percent of CPU this job got: 0%
	Elapsed (wall clock) time (h:mm:ss or m:ss): 0:00.20
```

The `sleep 0.2` stands in for a real step. The report continues
past what is shown, and its most useful line for a researcher is
lower down, "Maximum resident set size (kbytes)", which tells
you the peak memory a step needed, the number that decides
whether a job fits on your laptop or has to move to a bigger
machine. The resource figures vary from run to run; capture them
once as a record, not as a reproducible constant.

::: {.callout-note .divergence title="DIVERGENCE"}
The `-v` flag is GNU `time`, which macOS does not ship. The Mac
has a BSD `/usr/bin/time` whose long report is `-l`, not `-v`,
and whose layout differs (captured on the author's Mac,
`transcripts/ch10-bsd-mac.txt`):

```bash
$ /usr/bin/time -l sleep 0.2
        0.20 real         0.00 user         0.00 sys
             1196032  maximum resident set size
```

The numbers mean the same things, but the flag and the format
are not portable, and to complicate it further, `time` is also a
shell keyword, so `time cmd` and `/usr/bin/time cmd` are
different programs. Prefer the explicit full path
`/usr/bin/time` when you want the resource report, and remember
it is `-v` on Linux and `-l` on macOS. When you need one command
that runs unchanged on both, the shell keyword `time` (no path)
gives a short real/user/sys line on either.
:::

## Pin the environment: `LC_ALL` and `TZ` {.tier-beginner}

A script can be correct and still give two different answers on
two machines, because some tools read their behavior from the
environment rather than from your code. Two variables cause most
of the drift in research work. Chapter 8 showed the first,
`LC_ALL`: the locale sets `sort` order and therefore any
checksum taken over sorted output, so a script whose result must
be byte-identical everywhere pins it, `export LC_ALL=C`, to force
plain byte order regardless of the machine's language settings.
The second is the time zone, `TZ`, which changes how `date`
renders a timestamp. The same instant printed under two zones is
two different strings:

```bash
$ date -u -d @1700000000 +"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M %Z"
2023-11-14 22:13 UTC
$ TZ=America/New_York date -d @1700000000 +"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M %Z"
2023-11-14 17:13 EST
```

Same moment (a fixed Unix timestamp), two wall-clock strings,
because the zone differs by five hours. A script that stamps its
output files or parses dates will produce different results on a
laptop set to New York and a server set to UTC unless it pins
the zone, `export TZ=UTC`, so every machine agrees. Together
with the shebang and `set -euo pipefail`, these two lines are the
standard header this book puts at the top of a reproducible
script.

::: {.callout-important .reproducibility title="REPRODUCIBILITY"}
The reproducible-script header is four lines, and every line
earns its place:

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail
export LC_ALL=C
export TZ=UTC
```

The shebang fixes the interpreter, `set -euo pipefail` makes the
script stop at the first failure instead of writing a wrong
answer, and `LC_ALL=C` with `TZ=UTC` strip out the two
environment settings that would otherwise let sort order, date
strings, and any checksum taken over them depend on the machine.
Pin them whenever text the locale controls, a sort order, a
formatted date, a checksum over either, enters your outputs.
(The running example's own locked hash in Chapter 12 is stable
for a different reason, it digests rounded numeric bytes rather
than locale-sensitive text; but the instinct is the same, make
the result independent of where the script ran.) Use this header
for a reproducible shell script, and reach for the `LC_ALL`/`TZ`
pins whenever locale- or zone-sensitive text, a sort order, a
date, a checksum over either, enters your outputs. A script that
only shuffles numeric files may not need them, so pin
deliberately rather than by reflex, and note the exception when
you skip them.
:::

The date example uses a divergence worth naming. Reading a Unix
timestamp with `date -d @1700000000` is the GNU form; BSD `date`
on macOS spells it `date -r 1700000000`, and `-d` there means
something else entirely. It is the same `sed`/`date`/`readlink`
BSD-versus-GNU split from Chapter 5, and the portable move for a
script that must run on both is to pin `TZ` and avoid
platform-specific `date` parsing, or to compute timestamps in a
language layer (Python, R) whose date handling you control.

## Putting it together: a `verify.sh` for the project {.tier-advanced}

The pieces of this chapter combine into a small script the
running example actually needs: a guard that checks the raw data
is exactly what the analysis expects before any step runs. It
pins the environment, checks each raw file against a locked
checksum, fails loudly if a byte has drifted, and leaves a log.
Here it is, and its clean run:

```bash
$ cat verify.sh
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Fail loudly and reproduce identically: check the raw data
# against a locked checksum manifest before any analysis runs.
set -euo pipefail
export LC_ALL=C TZ=UTC
trap 'echo "verify.sh: failed at line $LINENO (exit $?)" >&2' ERR

cd "$(dirname "$0")"
echo "verifying raw data against the locked manifest"
sha256sum -c data/raw/SHA256SUMS
echo "verify.sh: data OK"
$ cat data/raw/SHA256SUMS
8648c3be59440b8be87b9a473a10a029b3e50f33a7af7bd454445de6fd31c7af  data/raw/factors.csv
5056f91931a3c99e95aa79125c22fc4bc025c49210c99ff52b9dcef6352a92af  data/raw/firm_panel.csv
$ ./verify.sh 2>&1 | tee verify.log
verifying raw data against the locked manifest
data/raw/factors.csv: OK
data/raw/firm_panel.csv: OK
verify.sh: data OK
$ cat verify.log
verifying raw data against the locked manifest
data/raw/factors.csv: OK
data/raw/firm_panel.csv: OK
verify.sh: data OK
```

Every line of this chapter is in that header: the `env bash`
shebang, `set -euo pipefail`, the pinned `LC_ALL`/`TZ`, and an
`ERR` trap that names the failing line. The body reads a manifest
of expected SHA-256 checksums, one per raw file, and asks
`sha256sum -c` to confirm each still matches; `tee` keeps a log
of the run. When the data is intact, every file reports `OK` and
the script exits 0. Now let a single byte of one input change,
and the guard does its job:

```bash
$ printf "\n# tampered\n" >> data/raw/factors.csv
$ ./verify.sh
verifying raw data against the locked manifest
data/raw/factors.csv: FAILED
data/raw/firm_panel.csv: OK
sha256sum: WARNING: 1 computed checksum did NOT match
verify.sh: failed at line 10 (exit 1)
$ echo $?
1
```

`sha256sum -c` marks the changed file `FAILED`, prints a
warning, and returns nonzero; `set -e` stops the script, the
`ERR` trap reports line 10, and the exit status is `1`, so a
Makefile or a collaborator running this first would halt before
analyzing corrupted input. This file-level check is the
lightweight sibling of Chapter 12's `make check`, which
re-derives the pipeline's *content* hash from the numbers
themselves; `verify.sh` guards the raw bytes, `make check` guards
the computed result, and both fail loudly so a reproducibility
problem stops the build instead of riding through it.

## Try it yourself {.unnumbered}

These use the asset-pricing project from Chapter 6. Do the
throwaway steps in `/tmp`, and keep a copy of the raw data so you
can restore it.

1. Add the header. Take one of the project's driver steps, or
   write a two-line script that copies a file, and give it the
   full header: `#!/usr/bin/env bash`, `set -euo pipefail`,
   `export LC_ALL=C`, `export TZ=UTC`. Make it executable with
   `chmod +x` and run it.

2. Watch it stop. Add a line that reads a file that does not
   exist, run the script, and confirm it stops there with a
   nonzero `echo $?` instead of running the lines after it. Then
   delete `set -e` from the header and watch the same script
   march past the failure; explain which output is now a lie.

3. Trap the failure. Add `trap 'echo "failed at line $LINENO"
   >&2' ERR` under the header and rerun the failing version;
   confirm it names the line. Add a second `trap ... EXIT` that
   removes a scratch file and confirm it runs on both success
   and failure.

4. Lint it. Run `shellcheck` on a script that uses an unquoted
   `$var` and fix what it flags (expect `SC2086`); then run
   `shfmt -d` on a carelessly indented script and apply the
   change with `shfmt -w`. Explain what each tool did and what
   it did not.

5. Pin and checksum, on a scratch copy. Copy the raw data to a
   throwaway dir first (`cp -r data/raw /tmp/verify-demo`) so the
   real `data/raw/` is never touched (Chapter 6's immutability
   rule). In the copy, write a `verify.sh` that pins `LC_ALL`/`TZ`
   and runs `sha256sum -c` against a manifest you build there
   (`cd /tmp/verify-demo && sha256sum *.csv > SHA256SUMS`), and
   `tee`s a log. Append a byte to one *copied* file, rerun, and
   confirm the script fails loudly with a nonzero exit; delete the
   scratch dir when done. Explain how this differs from Chapter
   12's `make check`.
